title: Postgres index types and their use
description: btree, gin, gist, brin, and hash each fit different predicates, and an index only helps a sargable query.
body: Use when deciding whether and how to index. btree serves equality and ordered range scans and backs most indexes; gin indexes the contents of composite values (jsonb containment, arrays, tsvector full-text); gist supports overlap, ranges, and nearest-neighbor (geometry, ranges); brin gives a tiny index over physically-ordered data like append-only timestamps. An index helps only if the predicate is sargable — wrapping a column in a function (lower(col)=...) defeats it unless you build a matching expression index. Do not index a column you never filter or join on. Actions: pick by predicate and verify with EXPLAIN. Validation signals include the planner choosing the index. Risk boundary: over-indexing a write-heavy table slows writes and bloats storage.
